questions.question: Explain the difference between using [] and [[]] when accessing elements from a list in R. Give an example.
keyPoints: [] returns a sub-list (still a list object)
[[]] returns the actual element itself (the object inside)
Using $ with named elements returns the element directly
Example: my_list[[1]] returns the element, my_list[1] returns a list containing that element

questions.question: How do you create a named list in R? Explain with code example.
keyPoints: Use names() function to assign names to list elements
Or use name=value syntax inside list() function
Named lists allow access via $ operator
Example: person <- list(name="John", age=30, city="Delhi")

questions.question: Write R code to add a new element to an existing list and remove an element from a list.
keyPoints: Add element: my_list[[n+1]] <- new_element or my_list$new_name <- value
Remove element: my_list[[n]] <- NULL removes the element
Setting to NULL shifts subsequent indices
Alternative: Use subsetting to keep desired elements

questions.question: Explain the purpose of lapply() and sapply() functions with respect to lists.
keyPoints: lapply() applies a function to each list element and always returns a list
sapply() does the same but attempts to simplify the result
sapply may return vector, matrix, or list depending on simplification
Both are used for iterating over list elements without explicit loops

questions.question: What are nested lists in R? How do you access elements in a nested list?
keyPoints: Nested lists contain other lists as their elements
Hierarchical structure like a tree
Access using multiple [[]] operators
Example: my_list[[1]][[2]] accesses element 2 within the first sub-list

questions.question: How does unlist() function work? What are its limitations?
keyPoints: unlist() converts a list into an atomic vector
It flattens all elements into one vector
Elements must be coercible to the same type (or will be coerced)
May lose information if elements have different types (coercion happens)

questions.question: Why are data frames considered specialized lists in R? Explain.
keyPoints: Data frames are lists with equal-length vectors as elements
Each column of a data frame is actually a list element
They combine list flexibility with tabular structure
All columns must have same number of rows (list elements have equal length)

questions.question: Create a list representing a student record with name, roll number, marks (subjects as vector), and grades (subjects as vector). Write R code to: (a) Create the list with named components (b) Access and display the student's name (c) Add a new element for attendance (d) Modify the marks (e) Remove the grades element.
keyPoints: Create list with list(name="...", roll=..., marks=c(...), grades=c(...))
Access name using $ or [[]]
Add attendance using $ operator or [[n+1]]
Modify marks by reassigning: list$marks <- new_marks
Remove grades by setting to NULL: list$grades <- NULL

questions.question: Write a complete R program that: (a) Creates three lists (b) Combines them into one list (c) Uses lapply() to find the length of each element (d) Uses sapply() to check if each element is numeric (e) Demonstrates the difference in their return types.
keyPoints: Create lists with different elements
Combine using c(list1, list2, list3)
lapply(my_list, length) returns list of lengths
sapply(my_list, is.numeric) returns vector of logical values
Demonstrate: lapply returns list, sapply returns simplified vector/matrix

questions.question: Explain the following functions with examples: is.list(), str(), unlist(), names(). Show how each is used with a list.
keyPoints: is.list() returns TRUE/FALSE for list type check
str() shows compact structure of list
unlist() converts list to vector
names() gets or sets element names
Provide code examples for each function demonstrating usage

questions.question: Write R code to demonstrate creating a nested list representing a department with multiple courses, where each course has its own list of students. Write functions to: (a) Add a new student to a specific course (b) Display all students in a course (c) Count total students in the department.
keyPoints: Create nested list: dept <- list(course1=list(students=c(...)), course2=list(...))
Add student: dept$course1$students <- c(dept$course1$students, "new_student")
Display: print(dept$course1$students)
Count: sum(sapply(dept, function(x) length(x$students)))
